class.name: PlatformVideoStream
class.brief_description: [VideoStream] resource for hardware-decoded, zero-copy native video playback.
class.description: A [VideoStream] that plays a native video clip through the platform's hardware decoder (AVFoundation on macOS, Windows Media Foundation on Windows) and presents frames to the GPU with no CPU copy. The decoded NV12 surface is imported via [code]RenderingDevice.texture_create_from_extension[/code] and converted to RGB in a single compute pass into an engine-owned [Texture2DRD] returned from the playback's texture; Godot never samples the decoder surface directly. Drop a recognised clip ([code].mp4[/code], [code].mov[/code], [code].m4v[/code]) onto a stock [VideoStreamPlayer] to play it. Requires Godot 4.4+ and a [b]Forward+[/b] or [b]Mobile[/b] renderer (RenderingDevice); the Compatibility/OpenGL renderer is not supported. The v1 decode scope covers H.264 and HEVC (Main/Main10) video, MP4/MOV containers, NV12 / x420 / BT.709 / BT.2020 / PQ / HLG, and AAC stereo audio. 10-bit sources negotiate a matching 10-bit decoder output — [code]x420[/code] biplanar on macOS, [code]P010[/code] on Windows — for full precision; the conversion shader normalises 10-bit payloads in 16-bit GPU words. On Windows, the default CPU-Copy Import Path (stock Vulkan driver) reaches the GPU via a readback rather than the zero-copy import macOS uses. PQ/HLG clips tone-map to watchable SDR by default, or output scene-linear HDR when the stream's output mode is set to HDR. VP9/AV1 and multi-track audio are not in scope.

method.name: get_color_info
method.description: Returns a [Dictionary] with the parsed/negotiated colorimetry metadata for the most recently opened clip. The dictionary contains the following keys: [code]matrix[/code] (int): YCbCr matrix — 0=Unspecified, 1=BT.709, 2=BT.601, 3=BT.2020. [code]primaries[/code] (int): Color primaries — 0=Unspecified, 1=BT.709, 2=BT.601-625 (PAL), 3=BT.601-525 (NTSC), 4=BT.2020, 5=DCI-P3. [code]transfer[/code] (int): Transfer function — 0=Unspecified, 1=BT.709, 2=PQ (SMPTE ST 2084), 3=HLG. [code]range[/code] (int): Video range — 0=Unspecified, 1=Video (limited), 2=Full. [code]bit_depth[/code] (int): Bits per channel (8 for 8-bit sources, 10 for 10-bit sources). Untagged clips (no embedded colorimetry metadata) return BT.709, video-range defaults, which is pixel-identical to the legacy hard-coded conversion shader. Must be called after [method VideoStreamPlayback.play] has started, i.e. after the stream has been opened by [VideoStreamPlayer].

method.name: hdr_decode_supported
method.description: Returns [code]true[/code] when the current platform supports hardware decoding of 10-bit (and HDR) video, matching the source's bit depth instead of down-converting to 8-bit. On macOS, [method hdr_decode_supported] always returns [code]true[/code] because the Metal-accelerated VideoToolbox can produce 10-bit biplanar surfaces (x420) that the Metal surface importer imports directly (zero-copy). On Windows, it also returns [code]true[/code]: Media Foundation negotiates 10-bit (P010) decoder output for 10-bit HEVC sources, and the default CPU-Copy Import Path (stock Vulkan driver) splits it into R16/RG16 planes and reaches the GPU via a readback rather than a zero-copy import. The zero-copy D3D12/DXGI Import Paths do not yet handle P010 and will drop 10-bit frames on that path — a known limitation, not a concern for the default configuration. On Linux, this returns [code]false[/code] — only 8-bit NV12 decode is supported there in this release. Call this before loading a clip to decide whether to offer HDR playback options in the UI, or to present an informative message when the clip's bit depth exceeds the platform's decode capability.
